comments.text: This seems relevant. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

It seems like the second question has been claimed to be solved in the affirmative. From page 8: "Indeed, in forthcoming work, Milojević, Pokrovskiy, Sudakov and [Montgomery] have been able to answer, for large $d$, a question of Erdős [13] by giving an exact extremal result corresponding to Theorem 2.3. That is, there is some $d_{0}$ such that for every $d\ge d_{0}$ among the $n$-vertex graphs $G$ with at least $d(n-d)$ edges, $\sum_{l\in C(G)}\frac{1}{l}$ is minimised exactly by the $n$-vertex graph with every possible edge between a set of $d$ vertices and a set of $n-d$ vertices, and no other edges." (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

There are some extremal graph theory questions for which proving it is true for small examples, even once some asymptotic case is proven, is still a huge challenge. Here I guess the statement is also true for small $d$. But knowing it for large $d$ is of course a good step forward
